title: Paper 1 — productive writing skills
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to mastering the IB Spanish B SL Paper 1 productive writing task. It breaks down the assessment criteria, explains how to choose and deconstruct a prompt, and offers strategies for structuring responses and using sophisticated language for key text types. The focus is on achieving top marks by aligning with examiner expectations.
learningObjectives: Understand and apply the three assessment criteria (Language, Message, Conceptual Understanding) for Paper 1.
Analyse and select the most suitable writing prompt from the three options provided.
Structure a written response logically, using appropriate conventions for different text types (e.g., email, blog, article).
Employ a wide range of vocabulary and complex grammatical structures relevant to the five IB themes.
Develop and justify ideas clearly and coherently to fulfil the communicative purpose of the task within the word count.

simpleExplanation.title: Paper 1: From Prompt to Perfection
simpleExplanation.summary: Paper 1 is your chance to showcase your writing skills in Spanish. You'll choose one of three prompts and write a 250-400 word response in a specific format, like an email or a blog post, based on one of the five course themes.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a professional communicator. A client gives you three different assignments: write a formal letter to a CEO, a blog post for teenagers, or a speech for a community event. Your job isn't just to write well, but to adapt your tone, style, and structure perfectly for each specific audience and purpose. Paper 1 tests your ability to be that versatile and effective communicator in Spanish.
steps: Deconstruct the Prompt: Before writing, identify the 4 Ps: 'Prompt' (key question), 'Purpose' (to inform, persuade, etc.), 'Public' (the audience), and 'Presentation' (the text type). This dictates your register and structure.

Plan Your Structure: Create a quick 3-part outline: an introduction stating your purpose, 2-3 body paragraphs developing your main points with examples, and a conclusion that summarises and provides a final thought.

Write with Purpose: Draft your response, focusing on fulfilling the task. Use varied vocabulary (Criterion A), develop your ideas clearly (Criterion B), and adhere to the text type conventions (Criterion C).

Review and Refine: Leave 5 minutes to proofread. Check for common grammatical errors (gender, verb conjugations), enhance vocabulary with synonyms, and ensure your message is coherent and directly answers the prompt.

flashcards.back: Assesses the quality and accuracy of your Spanish. Worth 12 points. Examiners look for varied vocabulary, complex grammatical structures, and a high degree of accuracy.
Assesses the development and coherence of your ideas. Worth 12 points. Examiners look for relevance to the prompt, clear development of ideas, and logical structure.

Assesses your ability to use the correct text type. Worth 6 points. Examiners look for correct use of register, tone, and structural conventions appropriate for the chosen format (e.g., email salutation, blog title).
The level of formality in your writing. It can be formal (usted), informal (tú), or neutral. This is determined by the audience in the prompt.
The specific features and layout of a text type. For example, a formal letter needs a date, salutation, and formal closing. A blog post needs a title and an engaging, personal tone.
Coherence is the logical flow of ideas. Cohesion is how you link those ideas using 'conectores' (linking words) like 'sin embargo', 'además', 'por lo tanto'.
Sentences that go beyond simple subject-verb-object. Examples include using the subjunctive, conditional clauses (si...), and varied tenses (e.g., pluperfect, future perfect).
Using specific, sophisticated words instead of simple, repetitive ones. For example, instead of 'bueno', use 'beneficioso', 'positivo', 'favorable', 'adecuado'.
Key conventions: 'Estimado/a Sr./Sra. [Apellido]:', use of 'usted', formal closing like 'Atentamente,' or 'Le saluda cordialmente,'. Purpose is usually to request, inform, or complain professionally.
Key conventions: Catchy title, direct address to the reader ('tú' or 'vosotros'), use of questions to engage, a more personal and conversational tone, and a clear closing or call to action.
Key conventions: Informative title, objective or balanced tone, structured paragraphs with a clear introduction and conclusion. Can be more formal than a blog.
Key conventions: Address to the audience ('Buenos días a todos'), rhetorical questions, persuasive language, clear signposting ('En primer lugar...'), and a powerful conclusion.

faq.q: What happens if I write more than 400 words?
faq.a: The IB states that the assessment should be based on the first 400 words. This means if your conclusion or a key argument appears after the 400-word mark, the examiner will not consider it, which could severely impact your marks for Criterion B (Message).

faq.q: How important is using complex grammar like the subjunctive?
faq.a: It is very important for achieving top marks in Criterion A. To move into the highest markband (10-12), you must demonstrate a 'command of complex grammatical structures'. Consistently using the subjunctive correctly is one of the clearest ways to show this. Practice trigger phrases like 'Es importante que...', 'Espero que...', 'No creo que...'.

faq.q: I always run out of time. How can I write faster?
faq.a: The key is not writing faster, but planning smarter. Spending 5-10 minutes creating a clear, simple plan (introduction, 2-3 body paragraphs with one idea each, conclusion) will prevent you from getting stuck or rewriting sentences. Knowing your structure beforehand allows you to focus solely on crafting high-quality sentences.

faq.q: What if I don't know much vocabulary for any of the topics?
faq.a: This highlights the importance of consistent revision across the five themes. However, in an exam, if you feel your vocabulary is weak for all topics, choose the prompt with the simplest task and the text type you know best. Focus on writing with maximum grammatical accuracy and perfect structure. A simple but accurate and well-structured response will score better than an ambitious but error-filled one.

sections.content: Welcome to your guide for IB Spanish B SL Paper 1. This exam is worth 25% of your final grade and lasts 1 hour and 15 minutes. You will choose one of three prompts and write a 250-400 word response. This paper isn't just about writing correct Spanish; it's about writing effective Spanish for a specific purpose and audience. This lesson will equip you with the strategies to excel under exam conditions.
Understanding the Assessment Criteria: Your Blueprint for Success
To get a Level 7, you must write for the examiner. This means understanding exactly what they are looking for. Your writing is assessed against three criteria:

items: **Criterion A: Lenguaje (12 marks):** This is about the quality of your Spanish. Top-band responses demonstrate language that is 'clear, effective, and varied', with a 'high degree of grammatical accuracy' and 'varied and complex structures'. This means avoiding basic errors and showing off your command of tenses, moods (especially the subjunctive), and sophisticated vocabulary.

**Criterion B: Mensaje (12 marks):** This assesses your message. The markband rewards responses where ideas are 'relevant, well-developed and organised'. Your writing must directly address the prompt, present a logical argument, and flow smoothly from one point to the next. Using cohesive devices is essential here.

**Criterion C: Comprensión conceptual (6 marks):** This is about choosing the right tool for the job. The prompt will specify a text type (email, blog, etc.). A top-band response demonstrates 'highly effective' use of the conventions of that text type, including the correct 'register and tone'. For example, a formal email that sounds like a casual blog post will lose marks here, even if the Spanish is perfect.

sections.content: Decoding the Prompt: Choosing Your Task Wisely

You will be presented with three prompts, each linked to one of the five prescribed themes (Identidades, Experiencias, Ingenio humano, Organización social, Cómo compartimos el planeta). Your first decision is the most critical: which one to choose? Do not rush this. Spend 5-10 minutes carefully reading all three options. For each prompt, ask yourself:

items: **What is the Text Type?** Is it a blog, a formal letter, a diary entry, a speech? Am I confident with its conventions?
**Who is the Audience?** Am I writing to a friend (informal), a school director (formal), or the general public (neutral/informative)? This determines your register ('tú' vs. 'usted').
**What is the Purpose?** Am I trying to persuade, inform, complain, advise, or reflect? This dictates the tone and the kind of language I should use.
**What are the Content Points?** The prompt will contain bullet points or instructions on what to include. Can I generate enough relevant ideas and vocabulary for this topic?

sections.content: Choose the prompt for which you have the strongest vocabulary and clearest ideas, and where you feel most confident with the text type conventions. It is better to write a brilliant blog post on a topic you know well than a mediocre formal letter on a topic you struggle with.
Mastering Key Text Types: Conventions and Register

Examiners consistently report that students lose marks on Criterion C. Mastering the conventions of the most common text types is a straightforward way to secure these 6 points. Let's focus on two frequent examples:

sections.question: Prompt: Eres un estudiante preocupado por el uso excesivo de los teléfonos móviles en tu colegio. Escribe un artículo para la revista escolar para concienciar a otros estudiantes sobre este problema y sugerir algunas soluciones. (Tema: Cómo compartimos el planeta / Identidades)
sections.solution:
### Model Response Breakdown (Article)

**Título:** `Móviles en el aula: ¿Conexión o Distracción?`
*This title is effective because it is engaging and immediately signals the topic and the debate within it, perfect for an article (Criterion C).*

**Párrafo de introducción:**
`En la era digital, el móvil se ha convertido en una extensión de nuestra mano. Sin embargo, ¿nos hemos parado a pensar en el impacto que su uso constante tiene en nuestra vida académica y social dentro del colegio? Este artículo busca explorar esta cuestión y proponer un equilibrio más saludable.`
*This opening uses a rhetorical question to hook the reader and clearly states the article's purpose, demonstrating strong organisation (Criterion B) and an appropriate tone for the target audience (fellow students) (Criterion C).*

**Estructura del cuerpo:**
1.  **Párrafo 1:** Develop the problem (e.g., lack of concentration, less face-to-face interaction). Use vocabulary like `la distracción`, `el rendimiento académico`, `el aislamiento` (Criterion A).
2.  **Párrafo 2:** Propose solutions (e.g., 'días sin móvil', designated phone zones, awareness campaigns). Use persuasive language and the subjunctive: `Sugiero que implementemos...`, `Sería beneficioso si creáramos...` (Criterion A & B).

**Conclusión:**
`En definitiva, no se trata de demonizar la tecnología, sino de aprender a controlarla. Asumir una responsabilidad digital colectiva es el primer paso para que nuestros móviles enriquezcan nuestra experiencia escolar, en lugar de empobrecerla.`
*This conclusion summarises the main argument and offers a final, reflective thought, providing a strong sense of closure (Criterion B).*

sections.content: Structuring for Impact: Coherence and Cohesion

A well-structured piece of writing is easy to follow and persuasive. Your plan should always include an introduction, body, and conclusion. The key to linking these parts smoothly is the masterful use of 'conectores' (linking words). These are the signposts that guide your reader through your argument. Simply listing ideas is not enough; you must show the relationship between them.

items: **Para contrastar ideas:** `sin embargo` (however), `no obstante` (nevertheless), `en cambio` (on the other hand), `por un lado... por otro lado...`
**Para mostrar causa y efecto:** `por lo tanto` (therefore), `así que` (so), `debido a` (due to), `a consecuencia de` (as a result of).
**Para concluir:** `en resumen` (in summary), `en conclusión` (in conclusion), `para concluir` (to conclude), `en definitiva` (ultimately).

sections.question: Prompt: Tu amigo español se va a mudar a tu ciudad por un año y te escribe un correo electrónico pidiendo consejos para adaptarse. Escríbele un correo electrónico informal dándole la bienvenida y ofreciéndole tres consejos prácticos. (Tema: Experiencias)
sections.solution:
### Model Response Breakdown (Informal Email)

**Asunto:** `¡Bienvenido a [Tu Ciudad]! Unos consejillos para ti`
*The subject line is friendly and informative, perfectly setting the informal tone (Criterion C).*

**Párrafo del cuerpo (desarrollando un consejo):**
`En cuanto a hacer amigos, ¡no te preocupes en absoluto! La gente aquí es muy abierta. Un consejo clave sería que te apuntaras a un club deportivo o a clases de algo que te guste. Por ejemplo, el polideportivo municipal tiene un equipo de baloncesto genial. De esta manera, no solo practicarás el idioma en un contexto relajado, sino que también conocerás a gente con tus mismos intereses. ¡Seguro que haces amigos en un abrir y cerrar de ojos!`
*This paragraph demonstrates excellent development of a single idea (Criterion B). It starts with a clear topic sentence ('Un consejo clave sería...'). It uses informal, encouraging language ('¡no te preocupes!', 'genial') and idiomatic expressions ('en un abrir y cerrar de ojos'), which shows a sophisticated command of informal register (Criterion A & C). The use of 'De esta manera' and 'sino que también' provides excellent cohesion (Criterion B). The use of the future tense ('practicarás', 'conocerás') is accurate and appropriate.*

sections.content: Word count is a guide, not a strict limit, but it matters. Writing under 250 words means your ideas are likely underdeveloped (low marks for Criterion B). Writing much over 400 means the examiner may stop reading, and you might miss your conclusion. Aim for the 300-380 word range to be safe.

sections.label: Test your skills with an exam-style Paper 1 prompt
